region_of_origin: South Asia / Central Asia (widely cultivated globally)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 maps to cantaloupe / muskmelon, which is the botanical equivalent of rock melon (Cucumis melo var. cantalupensis). Match is exact at species level.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of rock melon is approximately 150 g, equivalent to one medium wedge or one cup of cubed flesh, commonly consumed as a snack or fruit course.

nutrient_source.title: Rock Melon (Cantaloupe) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for rock melon (cantaloupe). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this primary regional source. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and select vitamins supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cantaloupe (NDB 09181) where HPB does not report those fields.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Cantaloupe / Rock Melon — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 65 (medium) for cantaloupe / rock melon is documented in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 8.16 × 1.5) / 100 = 7.96, rounded to 7.9. This confirms a low-to-moderate glycaemic load per standard serving despite a medium GI.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (16 mg/100 g) and high potassium (267 mg/100 g) yield a strongly favourable Na:K ratio consistent with DASH dietary principles.
hypertension.guidance: Rock melon is suitable for individuals with hypertension; its high potassium-to-sodium ratio supports vasodilation and blood pressure regulation, and a 150–200 g serving as whole fruit is preferred over juice to retain fibre.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI of 65 but low glycaemic load (~7.9 per 150 g serving) due to high water content (90%) and 0.9 g dietary fibre per 100 g moderating postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Rock melon can be included in a Type 2 Diabetes meal plan when portion is limited to approximately 150 g per serving to keep glycaemic load low; avoid canned or syrup-packed versions and monitor individual postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.05 g/100 g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, and meaningful potassium (267 mg/100 g) and vitamin C (36.7 mg/100 g) content support cardiovascular health.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Rock melon is a cardioprotective fruit choice; its very low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, high potassium, and antioxidant vitamin C content make it appropriate for regular inclusion in a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Rich in vitamin C (36.7 mg/100 g) and beta-carotene (pro-vitamin A, 169 mcg RAE/100 g), both antioxidants that may reduce oxidative stress; however, dietary fibre is below 3 g/100 g and no omega-3 fatty acids are present.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Rock melon provides antioxidant support relevant to rheumatoid arthritis via vitamin C and beta-carotene, but its anti-inflammatory contribution is limited; consume fresh and pair with omega-3-rich foods for more comprehensive RA dietary support.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rock melon (Cucumis melo var. cantalupensis), also known as cantaloupe or muskmelon, is a round to oval fresh fruit with a distinctive netted, pale-beige to tan rind and vibrant orange flesh. It is consumed raw, typically sliced into wedges or cubed, and is prized for its sweet, floral fragrance and juicy, soft texture when fully ripe. Rock melon is widely available in Singapore and across Southeast Asia, Australia, and globally, and is commonly eaten as a snack, fruit platter component, or light dessert. Nutritionally, it is low in calories (34 kcal/100 g) and fat (0.19 g/100 g), with moderate natural sugars (7.86 g/100 g) and a high water content (~90%), making it a hydrating, low-energy-density food. It is an excellent source of vitamin C (36.7 mg/100 g) and beta-carotene (vitamin A precursor, 169 mcg RAE/100 g), both potent antioxidants. Its high potassium (267 mg/100 g) and very low sodium (16 mg/100 g) profile aligns with DASH dietary principles for blood pressure management. The glycaemic index is medium (~65) but the glycaemic load per standard 150 g serving is low (~7.9), making it manageable for most individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when portion-controlled. Rock melon is naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher, and is free from common allergens.

Is rock melon suitable for a low-sodium diet?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo primary regional database with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, fatty acid fractions, and select vitamins cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 09181) for cantaloupe, which is the botanical equivalent. GI value of 65 confirmed via University of Sydney GI Database. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: Saturated fat (0.05 g), monounsaturated fat (0.0 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.08 g), trans fat (0.0 g), and detailed vitamin/mineral values (vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cantaloupe (NDB 09181) where HPB Foodo does not report those fields; this is documented as an adapted match. Monounsaturated fat is reported as 0.0 g per USDA SR Legacy for cantaloupe — this is a confirmed near-zero value from the approved source, not an assumed zero. Trans fat is 0.0 g confirmed per USDA SR Legacy. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 g and vitamin D is 0.0 mcg — both naturally zero for a fresh plant-based fruit, confirmed per USDA SR Legacy. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg — naturally zero as this is a plant food. No -1 sentinel values remain unexplained: all fields are populated with values from approved sources. Rock melon is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; it is a generic fresh fruit of international provenance, so no mandatory local-language names apply under the multilingual rules. The input record listed 'rheumatoid_arthritis' with suitable=false but the reason text described antioxidant benefits; this inconsistency has been corrected — the condition is retained in additional_conditions with a nuanced moderate concern level reflecting both the antioxidant benefit and the absence of strong anti-inflammatory factors. GI source: University of Sydney GI Database lists cantaloupe GI as approximately 65 (medium); glycaemic load per 150 g serving = (65 × 12.24) / 100 = 7.96, rounded to 7.9.
